blurb: The largest and smoothest of our native sumacs: a fast, suckering shrub grown for spectacular scarlet fall color and erect cones of fuzzy crimson berries that stand right through winter.
seasons: Greenish-yellow flower panicles in early-to-mid summer; brilliant scarlet-orange fall foliage; erect cones of crimson fuzzy berries persisting through winter
wildlife: Pollinators at bloom; berries feed birds through winter; larval host for several butterflies and moths
deer: Somewhat deer-resistant; foliage and fruit are browsed when other forage is scarce

care.hardiness: Iron-hardy through Boulder's zone 5b–6a — rated roughly USDA 3a–9b — and the only shrub or small tree native to all 48 contiguous states, so it is thoroughly at home in the Front Range's plains-and-foothills climate and high-altitude sun.[1][3]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost or in early fall on a well-drained site; it transplants readily and is used widely for erosion control and quick screens. Note it is largely dioecious — only female plants bear the red berry cones — so set out a female (or a suckering colony, which will be one clone) if you want the fruit and winter interest. In pots the roots are far less cold-hardy, so water container plants and shelter them over winter.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade; the densest habit and most brilliant scarlet-orange fall color come in full Front Range sun.[1][3]
care.soil: Adapts to most dry soils, from sandy loam to the region's heavy alkaline clay; it only dislikes wet ground, flooding, and high water tables.[1][2]
care.water: Extremely drought tolerant once established — a genuine xeric shrub that needs little to no supplemental water here after the first season.[1][3]
care.spacing: Give it room: it spreads by rhizomes into wide clonal thickets often broader than tall, so site it where a colony is welcome and space plants 4–6 ft apart for a screen, farther if you want a grove.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed has a hard coat and internal dormancy — scarify (an acid soak aids germination) then cold-moist stratify about 30 days near 40°F, or sow outdoors in fall for natural stratification. Far easier: it roots from softwood and root cuttings and transplants readily from the suckers thrown up along its shallow rhizomes.[2][3]
care.bloom: Dense terminal panicles of small greenish-yellow flowers open in early-to-mid summer (roughly May–July here); on female plants they give way to erect, pyramidal cones of fuzzy crimson drupes that color up in late summer and persist right through winter.[1][3]
care.pruning: Prune while fully dormant in mid-to-late winter. It sprouts vigorously from the root crown and rhizomes, so it takes hard renewal beautifully: cut older, congested, or winter-damaged stems back low, or take an overgrown, bare-bottomed colony right down to the ground to rejuvenate it — it resprouts densely the same season. Light shaping to keep it in bounds can be done any time. The main pitfall is spread rather than mis-timing: every cut stem and severed rhizome tends to resprout, so pair pruning with pulling or cutting the rooted suckers at the colony's edge or it will keep marching outward.[1][2][3]
care.maintenance: Low care once sited. Its defining habit is aggressive suckering — rhizomes can push new shoots many feet from the parent and build dense thickets — so the ongoing job is edge control: mow, pull, or cut the outer suckers each season, or contain it with a mowed strip or root barrier if you need it to stay put.[1][2]
care.harvest: For sumac-ade, clip the whole red berry cones once they are fully colored in late summer to early fall, before heavy rain leaches the tart surface coating; they hang on the shrub into winter if the birds don't strip them first.[1][3]

edible.summary: The tart red berries are edible — the classic use is a tangy, lemonade-like drink ('sumac-ade') the sour fruit was long steeped in water to make.[3]
edible.parts: The ripe red, hairy drupes borne in the erect terminal cones are the edible part; their sourness comes from a tart coating (rich in malic acid) on the fuzzy hairs that dissolves in water.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Rub or steep the whole berry cones in cool water — do not boil, which draws out bitter tannins — then strain out the fruit and hairs and sweeten to taste for a pink lemonade-like beverage; the sour fruit can also simply be chewed to quench thirst.[3]
edible.caution: This sumac is completely safe and is NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix), which is a white-berried tree of wet swamps; Rhus glabra bears upright cones of RED berries and is harmless. As a rule only the red-fruited sumacs are used for the drink, never any white-berried look-alike.[1][3]
